entry.title: Re: DOX: Fields now identified that does not exist in schema
entry.content: You can see the label-name relationship in the help portal: https://help.sap.com/docs/document-ai/sap-document-ai/extracted-header-fields. All the fields named "senderXYZ" are the ones labeled "Buyer XYZ". Label is derived from the defaultExtractor in the schema, but it should not appear if you create a copy of the schema and edit the labels there or remove the defaultExtractor. Best regards, Tobias

entry.title: Output Parameter Determination Invoice Send To Customer Via Email In SAP S4 HANA Public Cloud

Kindly check and guide us.

Regards,

Ashish

entry.title: Re: Error in Chart of Dep
entry.content:
Regarding error #1: Bear in mind that for each currency type assigned in FINSC_LEDGER you must have a parallel area to each posting area in OADB.

In SAP KBA 0003002785 - Migration Precheck: Error AC264 and ACC_AA116; we bring a template on how your customizing should look like once it is aligned.

Regarding issue #2: For each ledger assigned to a Company Code in FINSC_LEDGER you must have a set up of Depreciation Areas in OADB assigned to it.

entry.title: SAP MDK 25.9: I can't set DatePicker control to empty and also custom change its format
entry.content:
Hello everyone,
I am developing an application using SAP Mobile Development Kit (MDK) and I have a form that contains two optional (non‑mandatory) date fields. For these fields I am using the DatePicker control (Control.Type.FormCell.DatePicker) with

"Mode": "Date".

Issue 1: DatePicker should start empty:
I need the DatePicker controls to be initially empty, without showing a default value (such as today’s date).
The user should explicitly choose a date only if needed.
However, the DatePicker always seems to default to the current date, or display “Invalid Date”.

I have tried setting the Value property to an empty string (' '); a blank space (" "); null and "dd.MM.yyyy".
None of these approaches worked as expected. The control still initializes with today’s date or shows an invalid value.
Is there a recommended way in MDK to keep a DatePicker empty by default for optional fields?

Issue 2: Date format (dd.MM.yyyy):
I also need the date format to be dd.MM.yyyy (day first, then month).
However, I am not able to change the format from the default one, and setting a custom format does not seem to work. 
Is it possible to force the format to dd.MM.yyyy?

Any guidance, best practices, or examples would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you in advance!

entry.title: Handling Cross Business Object Transactions in RAP Using Actions
entry.content:
Introduction

In the SAP RAP, most examples focus on single Business Object (BO) operations. However, real-world enterprise applications rarely operate in isolation. A single business transaction often impacts multiple BOs—for example, creating a Delivery should trigger Billing, updating inventory, and possibly financial postings.
In this blog, we explore how to implement cross-BO interaction using RAP Actions, where:

  • A Delivery BO triggers creation in a Billing BO
  • Both operations are executed within the same LUW (Logical Unit of Work)
  • Data consistency is preserved using RAP’s transactional buffer

Business Scenario

Consider a typical Order-to-Cash process:

  1. A Delivery is created for a customer.
  2. Once the delivery is confirmed, a Billing document must be generated.
  3. The system should:
    • Automatically create a Billing record
    • Update the Delivery status to DELIVERED
    • Ensure both operations succeed or fail together

Implementation Steps

STEP 1: Create a Data base Table 

Data base  table For Delivery Details  

@EndUserText.label : 'delivery details' 
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE 
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT 
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A 
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED 
define table zte_t_delivery_s { 
key delivery_id : abap.char(10) not null; 
customer_id : abap.char(5); 
material : abap.char(20); 
price : abap.char(10); 
delivery_date : abap.dats; 
delivery_status : abap.char(15); 
}

Data Base table for Billing Details

@EndUserText.label : 'Billing Details' 
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE 
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT 
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A 
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED 
define table zte_t_billing_s { 
key billing_id : abap.char(10) not null; 
customer_id : abap.char(5); 
delivery_id : abap.char(10); 
material : abap.char(20); 
price : abap.char(10); 
}

STEP 2: Create root CDS views for both tables

Root view For Delivery Details

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED 
@EndUserText.label: 'Delivery Details' 
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true 
define root view entity ZI_T_DELIVERY_S as select from zte_t_delivery_s 
{ 
key delivery_id as DeliveryId, 
customer_id as CustomerId, 
material as Material, 
price as Price, 
delivery_date as DeliveryDate, 
delivery_status as DeliveryStatus 
}

 Root view For Billing Details

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED 
@EndUserText.label: 'Billing Details' 
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true 
define root view entity ZI_T_BILLING_S as select from zte_t_billing_s 
{ 
key billing_id as BillingId, 
customer_id as CustomerId, 
delivery_id as DeliveryId, 
material as Material, 
price as Price 
}

STEP 3: Create Projection View 

  • Add UI Annotations
  • Add Action Button In Delivery Projection

Projection View For Delivery Details

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED 
@EndUserText.label: 'Delivery Details' 
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true 
define root view entity ZC_T_DELIVERY_S 
provider contract transactional_query as projection on ZI_T_DELIVERY_S 
{ 
@UI.facet: [{ id: 'deliverdetails',  
position: 1,  
label: 'Order Details',  
type: #IDENTIFICATION_REFERENCE  
} ] 
@UI.lineItem: [ 
{ importance: #MEDIUM}, 
{ position: 10, type : #FOR_ACTION, label : 'Available', dataAction : 'create_delivery' }, 
{ position: 1, label : 'Delivery ID' }]  
@UI.identification: [{ position: 1, label : 'Delivery ID' }] 
key DeliveryId, 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 2, label : 'Customer ID' }]  
@UI.identification: [{ position: 2, label : 'Customer ID' }] 
CustomerId, 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 3, label : 'Material' }]  
@UI.identification: [{ position: 3, label : 'Material' }] 
Material, 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 4, label : 'Price' }]  
@UI.identification: [{ position: 4, label : 'Price' }] 
Price, 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 5, label : 'Delivery Date' }]  
@UI.identification: [{ position: 5, label : 'Delivery Date' }] 
DeliveryDate, 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 6, label : 'Delivery Status' }]  
@UI.identification: [{ position: 6, label : 'Delivery Status' }] 
DeliveryStatus 
}

Projection View for Billing Details

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED 
@EndUserText.label: 'Billing Details' 
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true 
define root view entity ZC_T_BILLING_S 
provider contract transactional_query as projection on ZI_T_BILLING_S 
{ 
@UI.facet: [{ id: 'Billingdetails',  
position: 1,  
label: 'Billing Details',  
type: #IDENTIFICATION_REFERENCE  
} ] 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 1, label : 'Billing Id' }]  
@UI.identification: [{ position: 1, label : 'Billing Id' }] 
key BillingId, 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 2, label : 'Customer Id' }]  
@UI.identification: [{ position: 2, label : 'Custoomer Id' }] 
CustomerId, 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 3, label : 'Delivery Id' }]  
@UI.identification: [{ position: 3, label : 'Delivery Id' }] 
DeliveryId, 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 4, label : 'Material' }]  
@UI.identification: [{ position: 4, label : 'Material' }] 
Material, 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 5, label : 'Price' }]  
@UI.identification: [{ position: 5, label : 'Price' }] 
Price 
}

STEP 4: Define Behavior Definitions
 Delivery BO behavior

  • Enable CRUD operation
  • Declare a non-factory action

This Action is responsible for creating Billing data and updating delivery status

managed implementation in class zbp_i_t_delivery_s unique; 
strict ( 2 ); 
define behavior for ZI_T_DELIVERY_S //alias <alias_name> 
persistent table zte_t_delivery_s 
lock master 
authorization master ( instance ) 
//etag master <field_name> 
{ 
create; 
update; 
delete; 
action create_delivery result [1] $self; 
mapping for zte_t_delivery_s 
{ 
DeliveryId = delivery_id; 
CustomerId = customer_id; 
Material = material; 
Price = price; 
DeliveryDate = delivery_date; 
DeliveryStatus = delivery_status; 
} 
}

Billing BO behavior

  • Enable CRUD operations
  • use Late Numbering for auto-generating Billing Id
managed implementation in class zbp_i_t_billing_s unique; 
strict ( 2 ); 
define behavior for ZI_T_BILLING_S //alias <alias_name> 
persistent table zte_t_billing_s 
lock master 
authorization master ( instance ) 
late numbering 
//etag master <field_name> 
{ 
create; 
update; 
delete; 

mapping for zte_t_billing_s 
{ 
BillingId = billing_id; 
DeliveryId = delivery_id; 
CustomerId = customer_id; 
Material = material; 
Price = price; 
} 
}

STEP 5: Define Projection behavior 

Delivery BO projection behavior

projection; 
strict ( 2 ); 
define behavior for ZC_T_DELIVERY_S //alias <alias_name> 
{ 
use create; 
use update; 
use delete; 
use action create_delivery; 
}

Billing BO projection behavior

projection; 
strict ( 2 ); 
define behavior for ZC_T_BILLING_S //alias <alias_name> 
{ 
use create; 
use update; 
use delete; 
}

STEP 6:  Implement Behavior Logic

Delivery Class

CLASS lhc_ZI_T_DELIVERY_S DEFINITION INHERITING FROM cl_abap_behavior_handler. 

PRIVATE SECTION. 
METHODS get_instance_authorizations FOR INSTANCE AUTHORIZATION 
IMPORTING keys REQUEST requested_authorizations FOR zi_t_delivery_s RESULT result. 

METHODS create_delivery FOR MODIFY 
IMPORTING keys FOR ACTION zi_t_delivery_s~create_delivery RESULT result. 
ENDCLASS.
 
CLASS lhc_ZI_T_DELIVERY_S IMPLEMENTATION. 
METHOD get_instance_authorizations. 
ENDMETHOD. 

METHOD create_delivery. 
READ ENTITIES OF ZI_T_DELIVERY_S 
IN LOCAL MODE 
ENTITY ZI_T_DELIVERY_S 
FIELDS ( DeliveryId CustomerId Material Price ) 
WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys ) 
RESULT DATA(lt_delivery). 
LOOP AT lt_delivery INTO DATA(ls_delivery). 

"Create Billing Entry 
MODIFY ENTITIES OF ZI_T_BILLING_S 
ENTITY ZI_T_BILLING_S 
CREATE 
FIELDS ( DeliveryId CustomerId Material Price ) 
WITH VALUE #( 
( 
%cid = 'BILL1' 
DeliveryId = ls_delivery-DeliveryId 
CustomerId = ls_delivery-CustomerId 
Material = ls_delivery-Material 
Price = ls_delivery-Price 
) 
) 
FAILED DATA(ls_failed) 
REPORTED DATA(ls_reported). 

IF ls_failed IS NOT INITIAL. 
reported = CORRESPONDING #( ls_reported ). 
RETURN. 
ENDIF. 

"Update Delivery Status 
MODIFY ENTITIES OF ZI_T_DELIVERY_S 
IN LOCAL MODE 
ENTITY ZI_T_DELIVERY_S 
UPDATE FIELDS ( DeliveryStatus ) 
WITH VALUE #( 
( 
DeliveryId = ls_delivery-DeliveryId 
DeliveryStatus = 'DELIVERED' 
) 
). 
ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD. 
ENDCLASS.

Billing Class

CLASS lhc_ZI_T_BILLING_S DEFINITION INHERITING FROM cl_abap_behavior_handler. 
PRIVATE SECTION. 
METHODS get_instance_authorizations FOR INSTANCE AUTHORIZATION 
IMPORTING keys REQUEST requested_authorizations FOR zi_t_billing_s RESULT result. 
ENDCLASS. 

CLASS lhc_ZI_T_BILLING_S IMPLEMENTATION. 
METHOD get_instance_authorizations. 
ENDMETHOD. 
ENDCLASS. 

CLASS lsc_ZI_T_BILLING_S DEFINITION INHERITING FROM cl_abap_behavior_saver. 
PROTECTED SECTION. 
METHODS adjust_numbers REDEFINITION. 
METHODS cleanup_finalize REDEFINITION. 
ENDCLASS. 

CLASS lsc_ZI_T_BILLING_S IMPLEMENTATION. 
METHOD adjust_numbers. 
SELECT FROM ZTE_T_Billing_S FIELDS MAX( billing_id ) INTO (ls_mat_num). 
DATA(lv_num) = ls_mat_num+3(4). 
LOOP AT mapped-zi_t_billing_s ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_material>). 
lv_num += 1. 
<fs_material>-BillingId = |BIL{ lv_num ALPHA = IN WIDTH = 4 }|. 
ENDLOOP. 
ENDMETHOD. 
METHOD cleanup_finalize. 
ENDMETHOD. 
ENDCLASS.

STEP 7: Service Defination

Expose Both Behavior objects

@EndUserText.label: 'Delivery and billing details' 
define service ZSD_T_DETAILS { 
expose ZC_T_DELIVERY_S; 
expose ZC_T_BILLING_S; 
}

STEP 8 : Service Binding

  • create service binding
  • Activate and publish

STEP 9: RESULT

Create a record and click on the Action Button the delivery Status will be updated and Billing records will be created 

Billing details when Action is trigged and Delivery status updated to Delivery

Conclusion

Cross Business Object operations in RAP are essential for building real-world enterprise applications. While RAP abstracts much of the complexity, designing such interactions requires a clear understanding of:

  • Transactional behavior
  • BO independence vs coordination
  • Framework-driven data consistency By leveraging RAP Actions and EML, we can safely orchestrate multi-BO updates without compromising on clean architecture or data integrity.

This example demonstrates how a simple Delivery trigger can drive Billing creation, showcasing a scalable pattern that can be extended to more complex business processes.

entry.title: Foreign currency contracts in RAR
entry.content:
• Foreign currency contract
  • 2 Methods:
  • Points to Note:
  • CONFIGURATION
  • Maintain Local currencies
  • Tables:
  • Setting up LC Calculation Method:
  • 2nd and 3rd LC Calculation- FI vs RAR
  • Maintain Exchange Rates
  • Define Condition Type for Exchange Difference
  • Define exchange difference (ED) account
  • Contract Balance- CA/CL
  • EXAMPLES
  • 1. Fixed Exchange Rate Method
  • Scenario A: First event- Billing- Period 1
  • Scenario B: First Event- Acceptance Date- Period 3
  • 2. Actual Exchange Rate Method
  • Scenario A: When 100% of the billing is done in Period 1- Historical Liability
  • Scenario B: When 50% of the billing is done in Period 1 and the remaining 50% in Period 2- Historical Liability Average
  • Scenario C: When 40% of the billing is done in Period 1, 30% in Period 2 and the remaining 30% in Period 3- Historical Liability Average
  • Scenario When 100% of the billing is done in Period 1, but invoice is due in Period 2- Historical Liability Rate
  • Scenario E: When 100% of the billing is done in Period 1, but invoice is due in Period 2- UR/DR
  • Scenario F: When 50% of the billing is done in Period 2 & 50% in Period 3, UR is created in period 1 & RV will be recognized at spot rate as there is no historical liability rate
  • BAdI
  • 1. Actual Exchange Rate Method
  • 2. Fixed Exchange Rate Method
  • Posting Process
  • Migration: Foreign Currency Handling
  • Invoice Cancellations & Returns
  • Exchange Rate Determination in case of changes
  • Rounding Difference for Fixed Exchange Rate Method
  • Conclusion

 

This blog is designed as a one-stop reference for handling foreign currency contracts. From the fundamental differences between Fixed and Actual exchange rate methods to deep-diving into BAdI implementations and Migration strategies, this will cover the end-to-end configuration and posting logic required to keep the books compliant.

Foreign currency contract

Contract whose document currency (transaction currency) is different from the company code currency (local currency).

2 Methods:

Method

Fixed Rate Method

Actual Rate Method

Version

Available since RAR 1.0

Available since RAR 1.3

Rate

Fixed rate determined on the first event in RA (first billing or fulfilment) or set manually beforehand

Spot rate or the historical liability rate/non-monetary liability rate

Revenue

Revenue recognized at that fixed rate

Revenue recognized on the historical liability rate or spot rate (if the historical liability rate isn’t present)

Contract Balance

Contract Balance calculated at that fixed rate

Contract balance based on actual rate of invoice/ invoice due and revenue recognition

FX gain/loss

Adjust FX gain/loss with fixed rate from billing

Adjust FX gain/loss from monetary asset when they are cleared by invoice/invoice due

 

Points to Note:

  • Fixed Exchange Rate Method only works with Classic Contract Management
  • For allocation, SSPs must be available in the document currency.
  • For contract combination, the contracts should be in the same document currency.

CONFIGURATION

Maintain Local currencies

Local currencies for CoCodes are maintained in OB22 in FI G/L

Tables:

We can find the local currencies in table T001 and T001A:

Table T001- local currency

Table T001A- additional local currencies

Only the currencies found in table T000 and T005 can be used as 2nd and 3rd local currencies:

T005- Index based currency and Hard currency defined for the country of the company code

T000- Group currency defined for the client

Revenue Accounting calculates and transfers amounts in all local currencies to FI.

Setting up LC Calculation Method:

On accounting principle level-

RAR contracts made before the change will keep using the previously set LC method.

2nd and 3rd LC Calculation- FI vs RAR

In SAP RAR, the Transaction Currency and 1st Local Currency are always calculated within the subledger. The resulting FI documents pull these values directly from RAR.

For the 2nd and 3rd Local Currencies, we have two configuration paths:

  1. Calculation via Standard FI Interface
  • How it works: RAR passes the 1st local currency to FI, and the FI interface calculates the 2nd and 3rd currencies using the exchange rate on the Posting Date.
  • Best for: Scenarios where secondary currencies are simply translated from the 1st local currency. This simplifies the RAR setup since we only need to validate the primary currency.
  1. Calculation via RAR
  • How it works: RAR calculates all currency valuations (1st, 2nd and 3rd) internally before sending them to the General Ledger.
  • Best for: Ensuring balance sheet consistency when you don't use standard translation at the reporting date. So that the three local currencies follow the same calculation logic.

Maintain Exchange Rates

Define Condition Type for Exchange Difference

Define exchange difference (ED) account

Tcode: OB09

Contract Balance- CA/CL

Non-monetary liability rate is used in Actual Exchange Rate Method

Non-monetary liability item: Liability due to billing/payment in advance.

Exchange rate when this liability is initially recognized is the rate used for revenue recognition

Monetary asset item: Asset due to revenue recognized in advance.

Realized FX gain/loss when payments are cleared

(RAR uses invoice due date as an approximation to payment date)

EXAMPLES

1.    Fixed Exchange Rate Method

Receivables revaluation in FI-AR occurs at period end. Receivable adjustment is revluated in RAR and ExDf posted accordingly.

Exchange difference when processing an invoice correction

Exchange rate for all fulfilments is fixed which is determined by the first event- GI/Invoice/Program A run. The IC posting is in the future exchange rate and the receivable adjustment postings in the fixed exchange rate, so the difference between the two is posted in the exchange difference account as gain or loss.

Set the LC Calculation Method as Fixed

First event determines the exchange rate for future periods revenue.

Scenario A: First event- Billing- Period 1

First event is billing on 01.01.2026, so the exchange rate on 01.01.2026 becomes the fixed exchange rate- 1.20

Note that 1.2 becomes the fixed exchange rate for future RV and CL

Exchange difference is the value of billing at 1.20 (fixed rate) subtracted from the value of billing at 1.30 (spot rate)

50/1.20=41.67                 50/1.30=38.46

41.67-38.46=3.21

Scenario B: First Event- Acceptance Date- Period 3

First event is Acceptance Date which is on 01.03.2026, so the exchange rate on 01.01.2026 becomes the fixed exchange rate- 0.92

Billing is still in period 1 and 2, but were invoiced after acceptance date was already processed

Note that 0.92 becomes the fixed exchange rate for future RV and CL

Exchange difference is the value of billing at 0.92 (fixed rate) subtracted from the value of billing at 1.20 and 1.30 (spot rate)

50/0.92= 54.35             

Period 1: 50/1.20=41.67           54.35-41.67=12.67

Period 2: 50/1.30=38.46           54.35-38.46=15.89

 

 

 

2.    Actual Exchange Rate Method

Set Document Currency to USD (foreign currency) as Local Currency is EUR (company code currency).

The contract is now a foreign currency contract. 

Scenario A: When 100% of the billing is done in Period 1- Historical Liability

The exchange rate for the billing becomes the historical liability rate, which for this example is 1.20. So now, CL and revenue in the next periods will be recognized with 1.20 exchange rate. ￼

Scenario B: When 50% of the billing is done in Period 1 and the remaining 50% in Period 2- Historical Liability Average

The exchange rate for 1st billing is 1.20 and for 2nd billing is 1.30. The exchange rate for revenue for period 2 and 3 and for CL for period 3, is average of the CL for period 1 and 2.

Scenario C: When 40% of the billing is done in Period 1, 30% in Period 2 and the remaining 30% in Period 3- Historical Liability Average

The exchange rate for billing in period 1, 2 and 3 is 1.20, 1.30 and 0.92 respectively, as per the rates maintained. RV and CL for period 1 is same as 1.20. For period 2, RV is the average of CL for period 1 and 2. For period 3, RV is the average of CL for periods 1, 2 and 3.

 

 

 

 

 

Scenario 😧 When 100% of the billing is done in Period 1, but invoice is due in Period 2- Historical Liability Rate

Revenue Accounting will post an exchange difference correction between the exchange rate at invoice issuance and on the invoice due date.

Billing is done at the exchange rate set in period 1, i.e., 1.20. Since the invoice is due in period 2, CL is created at the exchange rate of 1.30. Difference arising due to these exchange rate differences is posted to ED.

Now, since the first event from RAR standpoint is the creation of CL,1.30 (Historical Liability Rate) it becomes the exchange rate for future revenue and CL.

NOTE:

ED while using Actual Exchange Rate Method comes up if we use CA/CL because it uses invoice due dates. If we use UR/DR, invoice dates are used for calculation, so no ED comes up.

Scenario E: When 100% of the billing is done in Period 1, but invoice is due in Period 2- UR/DR

When the same scenario is run with DR, there is no ED. DR is recognized in the first period itself, even when the invoice due is in period 2.

Since the first event is creation of DR, which is in period 1, 1.20 becomes the exchange rate for the future revenue and DR.

Scenario F: When 50% of the billing is done in Period 2 & 50% in Period 3, UR is created in period 1 & RV will be recognized at spot rate as there is no historical liability rate

ED is the difference between billing and revenue+UR

BAdI

1.    Actual Exchange Rate Method

FARR_DISTRIBUTE_INVOICE- For contracts

  1. using Actual Exchange Rate Method and,
  2. where CA/CL is posted at POB Level

Invoice/Invoice due amount is distributed among POBs.

When price allocation is performed, invoiced amount on sending POBs are distributed to receiving POBs.

This BAdI is called during program A run.

2.    Fixed Exchange Rate Method

FARR_DIST_NET_CLCA_AMT_TO_POB- For contracts

  1. using Fixed Exchange Rate Method and,
  2. where CA/CL is posted at POB Level

 Distribution of CA/CL which has already been posted at contract level.

The system supports both Net and Gross reporting methods:

  • Net Case: Displays the total contract liability as a single figure on one side.
  • Gross Case: Offsets are shown individually. For example, instead of a single €90 liability, you would see a €120 liability and a €30 asset across different performance obligations.

Posting Process

1. RAI Processing

Billing & fulfilment events update the FARR_D_DEFITEM table only in transaction currency.

  • Billing Events: Trigger invoice correction postings.
  • Fulfillment Events: Trigger cost correction postings.

2. Transfer Revenue Program

Aggregates invoice and revenue amount incurred since the last execution

  • Distribution: Allocates the invoiced or invoice due amounts as required across POBs.
  • Exchange Difference: Calculates exchange difference
  • Local Currency Conversion: Determines the final revenue and cost recognition amounts in all relevant local currencies.

Note on FX Handling:  

  • For Flexible Rates, FARR_D_INV_FX_ED table is used to calculate CA/CL, aggregated by POB
  • For Fixed Rates, FARR_D_INVOICE table is used to calculate CA/CL

3. Calculate Contract Liabilities and Assets

Calculates contract balance and FX gain/loss

  • Balances: Calculation of CL/DR and CA/UR
  • FX Realization: Posts ED if an invoice is due within the current period

4. Revenue Posting Run

Posts FI documents to FI-GL and sends values to CO-PA

Migration: Foreign Currency Handling

Revenue contracts using fixed rate will only be migrated as fixed rate contracts.

To calculate the deferred revenue in LC, Revenue Accounting takes the exchange rate difference into consideration.

 

TC

LC

Invoice

300

340

Revenue

100

120

ED

 

-20

DR in Transaction Currency (TC) Calculation:

DR= 300 (Invoiced) - 100 (Recognized) = 200

DR in Local Currency (LC) Calculation:

DR= 340 (Invoiced) - 120 (Recognized) - (-20) (Exchange Difference) = 240

Invoice Cancellations & Returns

  1. Credits & Invoice Cancellation

When we reduce a receivable via a credit note, RAR determines the exchange rate based on current contract position:

  • If there is DR/CL: The credit first reduces this liability using its average historical rate. Any gap between the credit's value and the liability's historical value in local currency is posted as an Exchange Difference.

If there is NO DR/CL: The credit creates/re-establishes an Unbilled Receivable. This is valued using the historical rate of the recognized revenue. Difference b/w rate of invoice deduction and historical rate of revenue is posted as an Exchange Difference.

  1. Returns & Revenue De-recognition

When revenue is reversed (de-recognized), the system looks at the UR status:

  • If there is UR/CA: Revenue is de-recognized using the exchange rate of that outstanding UR. This ensures the asset and revenue are cleared out at the same valuation.
  • If there is NO UR/CA: Revenue is de-recognized at its historical rate. Deferred Revenue is then re-established using the historical rate of invoice/invoice due. Difference b/w de-recognition of revenue & deferred revenue is posted to Exchange Difference.

Exchange Rate Determination in case of changes

UR/DR

Change Type

What is Increasing?

Rate Used

What is Decreasing?

Rate Used

Invoice Increase

Deferred Revenue

Current Invoice Rate

Unbilled Revenue

Weighted Avg (Unbilled)

Invoice Decrease

Unbilled Revenue

Historical Revenue Rate

Deferred Revenue

Weighted Avg (Deferred)

Revenue Increase

Unbilled Revenue

Current Revenue Rate

Deferred Revenue

Weighted Avg (Deferred)

Revenue Decrease

Deferred Revenue

Historical Invoice Rate

Unbilled Revenue

Weighted Avg (Unbilled)

CA/CL

Change Type

What is Increasing?

Rate Used

What is Decreasing?

Rate Used

Invoice Due Increased

Contract Liability

Rate of the current invoice due

Contract Asset

Weighted average rate of contract asset

Invoice Due Decreased

Contract Asset

Weighted average rate of historical revenue

Contract Liability

Weighted average rate of invoice due

Revenue Increased

Contract Asset

Rate of the current revenue

Contract Liability

Weighted average rate of contract liability

Revenue Decreased

Contract Liability

Weighted average rate of historical invoice

Contract Asset

Weighted average rate of contract asset

Rounding Difference for Fixed Exchange Rate Method

When the transaction currency (TC) balance of a balance sheet account—such as a receivable adjustment—is 0, the corresponding local currency (LC) balance must also net to zero. Any residual amount remaining in the LC due to valuation discrepancies is classified and posted as a rounding difference.

The fixed exchange rate method has rounding differences in both Migration status and Productive status. 

Here 0.01 in RA has come up due to rounding differences.

Generate Rounding Posting Entries

FARR_ROUNDING_CALCULATION

Which will then post the difference of 0.01 to ED.

Conclusion

Mastering foreign currency contracts is a cornerstone of accurate financial reporting in SAP RAR. The key lies in a robust configuration and a clear understanding of your calculation methods. I hope this guide helps streamline your next implementation or troubleshooting session. 

Have you encountered unique challenges with rounding differences or LC calculations in your projects? Let’s discuss in the comments below!

entry.title: Re: Issue with Phase control for PM WOs in sap pm public...
entry.content:
Good day @vaishnavigujar 

Thank you for your question.

Based on what you mentioned, there might have been changes made in your system prior to that date in March. That change might have activated phase control codes, which is most likely root cause for your scenario. If that is the case, SAP provides custom logic extensibility to deactivate phase control codes on maintenance orders.

I suggest you check configuration Phase Control Codes for ZCNF an ZDNE first, especially the activation at creation setting for YA02. If that is unchanged, then you should review any active Custom Logic for maintenance orders, because that is the other standard place where these codes can automatically be added.

Here are references for more guidance

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/2dfa044a255f49e89a3050daf3c61c11/c7b1e894d4484d3ca16425634b17e09d.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/2dfa044a255f49e89a3050daf3c61c11/bb048da3cf164f4c8f98c2f2c9b7caa7.html?locale=en-US

Considering you mentioned that the behaviour started only from a certain date precisely 27 March 2026 onwards, I suggest you compare orders created before and after that date and check whether the order creation path or logic changed.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Recurring Deduction Integration in SAP EC: Challenges, Limitations & Workaround
entry.content:
Introduction

In one of our recent Employee Central implementations, we encountered a requirement to integrate data from a custom MDF object into the standard Recurring Deduction object. While this initially appeared to be a straightforward integration scenario, further analysis revealed system limitations that required a different approach. This blog outlines the challenge, solution design, and key learnings from this experience.

Business Requirement

The requirement was to: Extract data from a custom MDF object

Push the data into the Recurring Deduction object

Ensure timely processing aligned with payroll needs

Challenges Faced

The initial approach was to use standard Employee Central APIs.

However, during analysis, we identified a key limitation: the Recurring Deduction object does not have API visibility enabled in Object Definition.

We explored whether standard upsert mechanisms could be used as a workaround. However, the issue was not related to payload or mapping—it was due to the object not being exposed via API.

Solution Design

Given this limitation, we redesigned the integration approach instead of forcing a direct API-based solution.

The final design included SAP CPI and SFTP-based processing:

Data was extracted from MDF and sent to CPI

CPI transformed the data and placed it in an SFTP location

The file was then picked and processed into the Recurring Deduction object

Implementation Approach

To support this design, we implemented a two-job mechanism:

Job 1 (CPI): Extract MDF data and post it to the SFTP folder

Job 2 (Provisioning Job): Pick the file from SFTP and post it into the Recurring Deduction object

Additional Challenge

The client required the data to be available immediately (near real-time).

While SAP CPI allows multiple executions per day, the Provisioning job responsible for processing files from SFTP does not support the same level of scheduling flexibility.

Initially, this appeared to be a limitation that could impact the requirement.

Workaround Implemented

To meet the requirement, we implemented the following approach:

Created multiple identical Provisioning jobs (5 jobs)

Scheduled them at different intervals (staggered hourly)

Aligned CPI execution timing to ensure file readiness

Additionally, we maintained a "Modified Since Last Run" filter during data extraction. This ensured that only incremental data was processed and prevented duplicate records.

Outcome

With this approach:

Data was processed multiple times a day

Near real-time expectation was achieved

Duplicate records were avoided

The solution remained stable and aligned with system capabilities

Key Learnings

Not all Employee Central objects are API-enabled

System limitations should guide integration design

Direct approaches are not always feasible or optimal

Practical workarounds can effectively meet business needs

Incremental data handling is critical to avoid duplication

Conclusion

This scenario reinforced an important lesson: successful integration design is not just about technical capability, but about aligning with how the system is designed to operate.

Understanding these boundaries helps in building solutions that are both effective and sustainable.

entry.title: YCOA Account Groups - Verification of Usage and Difference between ABST and RECN
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

I'm working on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and preparing documentation for YCOA standard account groups. I've compiled descriptions based on typical GL account assignments, but I cannot find official documentation that defines the intended usage of each group.

**YCOA Account Groups - My Understanding:**

| Account Group | Text | My Understanding of Usage |
|--------------|------|---------------------------|
| ABST | Recon.account AP/AR | Reconciliation accounts linked to subledgers (Accounts Receivable, Accounts Payable, etc.) |
| ANL. | Fixed assets accounts | Tangible fixed assets (Buildings, Machinery, Land, etc.) |
| ERG. | Income statement accounts | Revenue and expense accounts (Sales Revenue, COGS, SG&A, etc.) |
| FIN. | Liquid funds accounts | Cash and highly liquid assets (Petty Cash, Bank Accounts, etc.) |
| MAT. | Materials management accounts | Inventory-related accounts (Raw Materials, WIP, Finished Goods, etc.) |
| RECN | ReconAcct ARAP Ready for Input | Reconciliation accounts allowing direct manual posting for AR/AP |
| SAKO | G/L accounts (general) | General GL accounts (Short-term Investments, Notes Payable, etc.) |
| SASL | G/L accounts (ledger-spec. OI) | Ledger-specific accounts |
| SECC | Secondary costs / revenues | Secondary cost elements for cost allocation (Allocation accounts, Transfer accounts, etc.) |

**Questions:**

**1. Is my understanding of these account groups correct? If not, which parts are incorrect?**

**2. What is the functional difference between ABST and RECN, and when should each be used?**
- What is the difference in posting behavior?
- Are there specific business scenarios where RECN is required instead of ABST?
- From SAP Note 2853117, I understand RECN is used with "Alternative Reconciliation Accounts" functionality - when is this needed?

**System Details:**
- SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
- Chart of Accounts: YCOA

Any guidance would be greatly appreciated!

Thank you in advance.

entry.title: Re: Relationship between CBC tenant and S/4HANA Cloud Pub...
entry.content:
Dear @byd,

The relationship is not 1:1. The standard model is one SAP Central Business Configuration (CBC) system/tenant with multiple workspaces and deployment targets, not one separate CBC tenant for each S/4HANA tenant. SAP documents that in a 3-system landscape you assign four deployment targets in CBC: Starter Customizing, Starter Development, Development Customizing and Development Development

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/doc/c38e987da8cf471f8888d703938f2ef1/SHIP3.0/en-US/SAPCentralBusinessConfiguration_ProjectExperience_en-US.pdf

Question 1.
One CBC system covers the landscape but not all listed tenants are direct CBC deployment targets. In 3SL, SAP says CBC deployment targets are only the starter and development side tenants. Test and Production are downstream tenants that receive changes through transport management. SAP explicitly states that dedicated tools and apps move transport requests from the development system into the test system and production system

More details above and below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/b249d650b15e4b3d9fc2077ee921abd0/1a02302321054e6ab4d9e7244156d31b.html

Question 2.
No. SAP documentation describes multiple workspaces and multiple deployment targets inside one CBC setup, not a dedicated CBC tenant per S/4HANA tenant. SAP also states that each tenant in the development system is linked to a corresponding workspace, which confirms the workspace-based model rather than a one-CBC-per-tenant model

More details above and below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/b249d650b15e4b3d9fc2077ee921abd0/e46f235a89ec40c6a1b54a712e955d41.html

Question 3.
Treat it as the same CBC system/context continuing from evaluation into implementation, with additional deployment target assignments, not as a new CBC tenant for each S/4HANA tenant. SAP describes the evaluation workspace for the starter phase and the implementation workspace for the target setup and it also states that the starter system is decommissioned 30 days after the production system is provisioned

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/doc/c38e987da8cf471f8888d703938f2ef1/SHIP3.0/en-US/SAPCentralBusinessConfiguration_ProjectExperience_en-US.pdf

 

Summary:

CBC to S/4HANA is 1:N at landscape level, not 1:1. In 3SL, CBC directly works with four deployment targets only while Test and Production are managed downstream through transports

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Smart Features (Smart Insights / Just Ask) with Fiscal Period Models in SAC – Any Workarounds?
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

I’m currently working on several FP&A and consolidation use cases in SAP Analytics Cloud where models are built using Fiscal Period / Fiscal Year dimensions (standard setup aligned with S/4HANA and finance processes).

As expected, we are facing the known limitation:
Smart Features (Smart Insights, Just Ask, Search to Insight) are not supported on models using fiscal time settings

Have you implemented any effective workaround or design pattern to enable or approximate Smart Features on fiscal-based models?

Specifically interested in:

  • Use of parallel calendar-based models → how do you manage reconciliation/governance?
  • Any approach leveraging Data Analyzer / calculated measures to mimic Smart Insights behavior?
  • Experience using Datasphere transformations to bridge fiscal ↔ calendar time for AI use cases
  • Trade-offs you’ve seen between accuracy vs usability

Context

In our architecture:

  • Planning and reporting models (Rev&Exp, Consolidation, Workforce, etc.) are fully fiscal-based
  • Data is sourced via SAP Datasphere and aligned with finance calendars
  • Business users (FP&A, Controllers) are increasingly asking for augmented analytics capabilities directly on these models

Appreciate any insights, patterns, or lessons learned from your implementations.

Thanks in advance.

SAP Analytics Cloud SAP Datasphere SAP Analytics Cloud, augmented analytics

entry.title: Create Transfer Orders Using BAPI L_TO_CREATE_SINGLE in SAP WM
entry.content:
This blog explains how to create a Transfer Order programmatically using the BAPI BAPI_L_TO_CREATE_SINGLE, with a step-by-step approach.

BAPI L_TO_CREATE_SINGLE is a standard SAP function module used to:

  • Create a single Transfer Order (TO) in WM
  • Move stock from source storage type/bin to destination storage type/bin
  • Handle both foreground and background execution

🧩 Key Import Parameters

Some important parameters of the BAPI:

  • I_LGNUM  --> Enter Warehouse Number
  • I_BWLVS --> Controls WM Movement Logic (999)
  • I_MATNR --> Enter Material Number
  • I_WERKS --> Enter Plant Code
  • I_LGORT --> Enter Storage Location
  • I_CHARG --> Enter Batch
  • I_ANFME --> Enter Quantity
  • I_ALTME --> Unit of Measure
  • I_SQIT --> Immediate TO Confirmation (If yes “X” else “Empty”)

Source Details

  • I_VLTYP --> Source Storage Type
  • I_VPPOS --> Source Storage Section
  • I_VLPLA --> Source Storage Bin

Destination Details

  • I_NLTYP --> Destination Storage Type
  • I_NPPOS --> Destination Storage Section
  • I_NLPLA --> Destination Storage Bin

Test Scenario

Step 1: Stock Overview (Transaction: LS24/LX02)

Step 2: Execute BAPI L_TO_CREATE_SINGLE Transaction: SE37)

Execute BAPI

Step 3: Transfer Order Created 

Since I_SQIT is not checked as “X”, TO created but not confirmed, let’s check in LT22.

Step 4: Confirm Transfer Order (Transaction: LT22/LT12)

Select the TO line and click on confirm TO background, status turns into green.

 

If you found this guide helpful, don't miss any other SAP MM WM blogs covering real-world automation, configurations tips, and best practices for procurement, inventory management, and warehouse management.

Click to view other SAP MM WM Blogs - SAP Community

 

Thanks,

Leelamohan Mannem

entry.title: SAP Business AI Capabilities Tile in SAP Readiness Check
entry.content:
Discover SAP Business AI Capabilities in Your SAP Readiness Check Results 

Explore AI-powered features available for your SAP S/4HANA landscape — and understand what’s needed to unlock them. 

SAP Readiness Check for SAP S/4HANA now includes a dedicated tile for SAP Business AI Capabilities. This tile gives transparency into SAP’s AI portfolio — including Generative AI, Agentic AI, and Joule-powered experiences — and shows which capabilities are available for your target SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition release. 

Whether you’re planning a conversion from SAP ECC to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, a migration from SAP S/4HANA (on-premise) to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, or an upgrade of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition to a newer release, this tile helps you answer questions like: 

  • What SAP Business AI capabilities are available to me? 
  • Do I need to upgrade to use them? 
  • What prerequisites should I consider? 

Who Is This For? 

  • Solution architects planning AI adoption roadmaps 
  • LoB owners evaluating which AI capabilities fit their target release 
  • PMO and readiness teams needing an exportable list for planning 

The tile assesses the release compatibility of  SAP Business AI Capabilities against your selected SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition target release. 

The dashboard tile includes three summary charts:

  1. By Key Product Area - Distribution of AI features across major SAP product lines. 
  2. By Product Sub-Area - The top 5 product sub-areas related to the product area “SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition”.  
  3. By Readiness Status - Distribution by the calculated Readiness Status.

 

Readiness Status Short description
Ready Your target release meets the minimum required version 
Upgrade Required Your target release is below the minimum required version 
Requires SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition Migrate from on-premise to unlock this capability 
Available for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition This SAP Business AI capability is available for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 
Not Assessed SAP Business AI capabilities in this product area are not assessed as part of SAP Readiness Check for SAP S/4HANA

 

Detailed View

Within the new SAP Business AI Capabilities detail page, you will find two sections. The chart in the top section shows the distribution of SAP Business AI capabilities by deployment model, Readiness Status, and by the manually selected scope status. You may use the chart for filtering the detailed table below. By default, the table is filtered by Product Area = “SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition.” You may adjust or remove filters to explore other areas.

The second section lists SAP Business AI Capabilities in a table.

Click a capability to view its Readiness Status, prerequisites, business mapping (functional area, business process context), and links to product documentation.

 

Manually Selected Scope Status 

You may mark capabilities as "In Scope" or "Out of Scope" to track your planning. For offline review and stakeholder alignment, export the table to Excel and share with the team as needed.

Questions to consider when reviewing the table can be: 

  • Which business areas (for example, Finance, Sales, Supply Chain) gain the most value first? 
  • Which prerequisites require lead time (release upgrade, service activation, licensing)? 
  • Which quick wins can be implemented to build momentum? 

Upgrade Scenario 

If you’re upgrading within SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, the tile shows additional context: 

  • "This feature is already available on your current release" — no action needed 
  • "This feature becomes available with your upgrade" — capability is unlocked by moving to your target release 

This helps you identify the business value of upgrading and which new AI capabilities you’ll gain. 

First release version of the tile (phase 1) -  Scope & Limitations 

This is  the first release version (phase 1) of the tile, focused on release compatibility. BTP service provisioning checks and usage-based recommendations are planned for later phases. 

How to Get Started 

  1. Run SAP Readiness Check for your system 
  2. Open the SAP Business AI Capabilities tile from the dashboard 
  3. Review capabilities by Readiness Status
  4. Explore details for capabilities of interest 
  5. Mark the scope and export it for planning discussions

Next Steps 

  • Is the capability ready to be used? Review prerequisites and plan activation 
  • Is an additional upgrade required? Consider targeting a higher release 
  • Is your target release on-premise, but you would like to use SAP Business AI Capabilities? Explore the migration to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition to unlock AI capabilities 

For more information, see: 

  • SAP Discovery Center – Business AI  
  • Help Portal – SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 
  • Road Map Explorer – use filters: Product = SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition; Topic = Artificial Intelligence https://roadmaps.sap.com/board 

As always, we look forward to receiving your feedback!
Your SAP Readiness Check Team

entry.title: Re: Integrating India GST Reporting, E-Invoicing, and E-Waybills with DRC Cloud Edition
entry.content:
We are currently on SAP S/4 HANA on Premise 2020, and for country India we are using E invoicing and E waybill with SAP integration Suite 

SAP S/4 HANA on Premise 2020-> BTP (SAP integration Suite ) -> GSP -> GST Portals

Now, we want to upgrade to SAP S/4 HANA Private cloud 2020, and also Activate SAP DRC for E document, Ewaybill and Statutory Reporting (Example GSTR1)... 

Option 1: SAP S/4 HANA on Premise 2020-> BTP (SAP integration Suite) -> GSP -> GST Portals

can we continue using SAP integration Suite? If yes still when SAP will support are the Pro's and cons.

Option 2: SAP S/4 HANA on Premise 2020-> BTP (SAP DRC cloud edition) -> GSP -> GST Portals

what are the advantages over SAP integration Suite.

Your comments are really appreciated ..

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602.2: Final What's New & Product Assistance available
entry.content:
With immediate effect, the final What's New information for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602.2 is now available.

To access the final What’s New information, please use the What’s New Viewer. In addition, the final Product Assistance (aka Application Help) for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602.2 is available. Please use the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Help Portal page to access it.

For you as a customer, we recommend to also use the Release Assessment and Scope Dependency (RASD) tool. The Release Assessment and Scope Dependency (RASD) tool provides you with a personalized and tailored view on What’s New information based on the activated and used scope within your system. The information for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602.2 will be available in RASD within the next few days. A valid Customer S-user ID is required for login. We recommend to use RASD to prepare for upgrades and updates of your system and to focus on the new and changed features that are relevant for you as a customer.

You can use the commenting feature for What’s New. With this feature, authors can evaluate your feedback and incorporate it into the documentation, if needed. We hope that by opening this channel of communication, we can learn how to further improve our What’s New content for you. To use this feature, you need to be logged into the Help Portal. The feature will only be available in the English version of the documents. To learn more about how the feedback process works in SAP Help Portal, see Giving Documentation Feedback.

entry.title: SAP Commerce - Custom Labels Overwritten When Running "Localize Types" During Update
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

I’m facing an issue with SAP Commerce during system updates, specifically when using migration data and running an update running system.

I noticed that some labels are being overwritten by standard SAP Commerce values. To address this, I added my custom labels in the createEssentialData method inside initialDataSystemSetup.

Here’s the behavior I’m seeing:

  • If I go to HAC → Update and select only Create Essential Data, everything works fine and my custom labels are correctly applied.
  • However, if I also select Localize Types, my custom labels get overwritten again by the default ones.

So my question is:
Why does the "Localize Types" option override labels defined in createEssentialData?
Is there a recommended way to persist custom labels so they are not reset during localization updates?

Any insights or best practices would be really appreciated.

Thanks!

entry.title: Re: Relationship between CBC tenant and S/4HANA Cloud Pub...
entry.content:
Hi @byd ,

Let's change a perspective to look at your questions.

The CBC is a central configuration tool, not only for the Public Cloud, but eventually for more SAP solutions. So it is one CBC for many solutions.

Within the CBC, you work on workspaces/projects, which is targeting to one specific configuration or development tenant in the Public Cloud. So it is 1:1 relationship between a workspace and a tenant.

In the 3SL, only the development system (configuration tenant 100 and development tenant 080) can be targeted/connected by the CBC.  The Test and Production tenants only receive transports from the development system. So they don't connect to the CBC directly. 

In the meantime, if you use Parallel Line, you can connect its configuration tenant 120 to a new workspace in the CBC as well.

At the same time, the CBC can have multiple workspaces without a target system, I call them dummy workspaces. You cannot deploy its content to anywhere.

Hope this helps you to understand the relationship between them.

Best,

George

entry.title: Third-Party Payments with SIPO
entry.content:
This blog post introduces business scenarios and explains how to process payment with SIPO in Czech Republic for SAP S/4HANA or SAP S/4HANA Cloud private edition. 

SIPO (Soustředěné Inkaso Plateb Obyvatelstva) that is used for direct debit of multiple payments through the Czech Post’s system. It is a national payment aggregation system operated by the Czech Post. This business concept relates to payments through a third party. 

Business Scenarios

Let’s start with the parties involved in SIPO payments.  

  1. Customers that have SIPO connection number pay on a regular basis 
  2. Service providers that offer services of utilities, multimedia, or internet, send payment requests to SIPO 
  3. Czech Post Office, or the SIPO operator, collects all payment requests, aggregates them into one payment, and distribute paid money by customers to service providers 
  4. Banks that serve as payment channels carry out direct debit from bank accounts

There are two business scenarios of payments involving SIPO: 

Scenario 1: You, as a service provider, post receivable items. After executing a payment run, you send a SIPO file, as an outgoing file, to the Czech Post for informing the customers to pay.

Scenario 2: Continuing with scenario 1, you, as a service provider, want to clear the open items by processing incoming SIPO files after your customer completes the payment. 

Prerequisites

Bank Account Settings

Since the payments with SIPO order are not directly paid out from the payer's bank account, instead it is paid out from the post office, it is necessary to create a fictive bank account and account ID for the post office.

Open the Manage Bank Accounts app.  
For your company code, create a fictive Bank Account and Account ID for the Czech Post. 

Define Parameters for Paying Out

Since payment is carried out through the third party (Czech Post),  you need to identify which payee whom the post office pays.   

Use the Define Parameters for Foreign Payment Transactions view (TFK012D) in the transaction SM30 to fill the service provider's SIPO number in the Customer number field.

Define Payment Methods 

Use the Define Payment Methods (ID: 101952) View cluster VC_TFK042Z in the transaction SM34. 

Create a payment method or use the pre-delivered format - CZ_PSTLPAYMENT_SIPO as the payment medium format for Czech SIPO payments.

Define Payment Lot Specifications

Use the Define Payment Lot Specification view ( V_TFKIDEXPC_ZK ) in the transaction SM30. 

Select the format type Czech Republic: Postal Payment Item (SIPO file) (CZ-POSTAL-PAYMENT-ZA) and complete the Bank statement settings section.  
Note: It is not required to configure the Czech Republic: Postal Payment Header (SIPO file) (CZ-POSTAL-PAYMENT-PZ). 

Define Interpretation Rules for Note to Payee

Use  the Define Interpretation Rules for Note to Payee view cluster (VC_TFK006) to assign keywords.

Procedure

  1. You post contract accounting receivable items. 
  2. Execute a payment run. The payment order then is created. 
  3. During the payment run, the system uses the payment format CZ-POSTAL-PAYMENT-SIPO to generate the payment file for the Czech Post. 
  4. You then send the payment file to Czech Post. 
    Czech Post informs the customer to conduct the payment.

Processing SIPO Incoming Payment Files 

After the customer pays, the Czech Post sends back the SIPO files.  

The SIPO files contain the following files:

  • SIPO header file – Includes the total settlement amount that matches the bank statement of the incoming payment 
  • SIPO item file – lists the clearing of open receivable items per customer’s individual SIPO number 

You have to import both files in the Manage Incoming Payment File (3rd-Party Payments) app (F1680).

  1. Open the Manage Incoming Payment File (3rd-Party Payments) app (F1680). 
    • Select Import and choose Third-party Payment.
    • Select Czech Republic: Postal Payment Header (SIPO file) and upload the SIPO header file.
    • Repeat the same operation and upload the Czech Republic: Postal Payment Item (SIPO file).

  2. Open the Process Imported Third-Party Payments app (F9000) to schedule a run. 
    • Select Third-Party Payment: Transfer data as the job template.  
    • In the Parameters, select Czech Republic: Postal Payment Item (SIPO file) (CZ-POSTAL-PAYMENT-ZA) as a format type.  
      Note: When processing the imported file, you only need to process the item files. (Header files are not required.)  
    • Schedule a run and you can see that the payment lot number is created in the log.

      If you select the Post Lot check box and you can directly process the payment order lot without additional operation.

  3. Use the payment lot and process it in the Process Payment (Order) Lot transaction (FP45) and clear open items of the customer. If you did not select the checkbox in the previous step.

Is this blog post useful to you? Leave your comments below to let us know your thoughts.

entry.title: SAP Commerce Cloud v2: Enhanced ES Service CQC Deployment Readiness
entry.content:
Introduction

We're excited to announce significant enhancements to our Enterprise Support (ES) Continuous Quality Check (CQC) for Deployment Readiness service for SAP Commerce Cloud v2. These improvements strengthen our commitment to ensuring successful deployments by adding critical cluster configuration validations and enhanced cutover planning capabilities. This enhanced service provides customers with greater confidence in their deployment readiness while focusing specifically on pre-go-live optimization to minimize risks and ensure operational excellence.

What's New in CQC Deployment Readiness

Enhanced Cluster Configuration Checks

Our enhanced CQC service now includes comprehensive validation of cluster configurations, with a particular focus on ensuring TaskEngine is properly disabled on Storefront Nodes,[2]. This critical check prevents performance bottlenecks and ensures optimal resource allocation across your SAP Commerce Cloud infrastructure. By validating this configuration early in the deployment process, we help prevent common issues that could impact storefront performance and customer experience during high-traffic periods.

The technical benefits include improved system stability, optimized resource utilization, and reduced risk of performance degradation during peak operations. This validation ensures your cluster architecture follows SAP best practices for production environments.

Improved Cutover Planning Verification

The enhanced service introduces robust verification mechanisms to confirm successful deployment completion[2]. These new capabilities include comprehensive validation of all deployment components, and confirmation that all services are functioning correctly post-deployment.

This systematic approach to cutover verification ensures smoother go-live processes by identifying and addressing potential issues before they impact production operations.

Focus Shift: Pre-Go-Live Optimization

Updated CQC Scope

Our CQC service has been strategically refocused to prioritize pre-go-live checks, moving away from post-implementation reviews to proactive deployment readiness validation. This shift ensures that potential issues are identified and resolved before they can impact on your production environment.

The updated approach emphasizes preventive quality assurance rather than reactive problem-solving, and reduces deployment risks and accelerates time-to-production.

Key Benefits for Customers

Risk Reduction: By conducting comprehensive pre-go-live validations, we significantly reduce the likelihood of deployment failures and production issues. The enhanced checks identify configuration problems before they impact your business operations.

Deployment Confidence: The improved verification processes provide development and operations teams with greater confidence in deployment success. Detailed validation reports offer clear insights into system readiness and highlight any areas requiring attention.

Operational Efficiency: The focus on pre-go-live optimization streamlines the deployment process and reduces the need for post-deployment troubleshooting. This approach minimizes downtime and accelerates the path to productive system operation.

Next Steps

Immediate Actions: Customers planning SAP Commerce Cloud v2 deployments should schedule their enhanced CQC service at least six weeks before their planned go-live date to allow adequate time for implementing recommendations.

Service Booking: Request the enhanced CQC service through the "Get Support" application in SAP for Me, selecting "Continuous Quality Check & Improvement Services" as the product and "Service request for a Cloud Solution" as the product function.

Timeline: The enhanced CQC service is available immediately for all SAP Commerce Cloud v2 implementations. SAP recommends booking the service four weeks before your go-live date to ensure sufficient time for review and implementation of recommendations.

Support Resources: For assistance with service requests or questions about the enhanced CQC capabilities, contact your local Customer Interaction Center (CIC) or reach out to the SAP Enterprise Support team.

Ready to ensure your SAP Commerce Cloud v2 deployment success?

Visit: Continuous quality check (CQC) and improvement services

entry.title: Re: incrementalMigrationJobNonLP failing intermittently w...
entry.content:
I suggest you to check how the DB Sync toolkit is executing the transformation scripts. The error suggests that the script is attempting to recreate the table MIGRATIONTOOLKIT_TF_TYPEINFO, which already exists. 

( There is already an object named 'MIGRATIONTOOLKIT_TF_TYPEINFO' in the database.) 

As a suggestion, you can verify:

whether the incremental job is running in parallel
whether initialization logic is being triggered more than once
whether any process is resetting or affecting MIGRATIONTOOLKIT_* tables

Please note: sap-commerce-db-sync - This repository is provided "as-is"; SAP does not provide any additional support.

SAP Services team is the creator and main contributor to SAP Commerce DB Sync, the team can provide assistance as a paid engagement. If you plan to setup SAP Commerce DB Sync for your project, we highly recommend involving SAP Services to ensure a successful implementation.

For more details, please refer to:-  - 2706322 - What is Support – What is Consulting: Cloud Solutions

entry.title: Re: Error in MD03 MRP run "in house production time i...
entry.content:
Why Case 1 vs. Case 2 behave differently is because

  • in Case 2 (Material 0022840001 / Plant 1173), the system is actively validating the processing time vs. base quantity relationship in the Work Scheduling view and throwing the “in‑house production time too long” error.
  • in Case 1 (Material 021475‑000 / Plant 1306) even if processing time > base quantity, the system does not throw the error. This usually points to different plant‑specific customizing or master data defaults.

So the recommended approach is to keep the earlier checks (Work Scheduling view values, routing consistency, plant‑specific settings). Additionally, compare MRP group, scheduling margin key, lot size procedure, and production scheduling profile between the two cases. The difference in these settings is most likely why Case 1 tolerates high processing time vs. base quantity, while Case 2 does not.

entry.title: Re: How is Column Store fetch faster than Row Store?
entry.content: This is a very clear and practical breakdown of why columnar storage fundamentally changes the economics of data access. The use of real byte‑level calculations makes the trade‑offs tangible, especially for teams who still assume indexing can compensate for row‑store limitations at scale. What stands out is how you highlight the hidden costs—random I/O, cache misses, and unnecessary row materialization—that developers often overlook. The comparison also reinforces why analytical workloads benefit so much from sequential scans, compression, and vectorized execution. It’s a great reminder that storage format isn’t just a physical detail; it directly shapes query performance, CPU efficiency, and system scalability. Posts like this help bridge the gap between database theory and real‑world engineering decisions.

entry.title: How to Use Custom Fields in Event-Based Settlement for Assets Under Construction
entry.content:
Co-author: H. Trefftz

Learn how custom fields can be used in the event-based settlement process from cost posting through to final asset reporting.

Since SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2508, you can use event-based settlement to settle investment projects to assets under construction.

Release 2602 takes this further by introducing custom field support throughout the entire settlement process. You can use app extensibility and define custom fields that you can then use throughout the whole event-based settlement process: You can post to the custom fields in the cost posting, for example, when posting an invoice. The custom fields are also posted to in the settlement postings: The custom field values are inherited when the WBS element is settled to the asset under construction (AuC) and also when the AuC is settled to the completed asset. Finally, the customer fields are also available in reporting. This means better tracking and more meaningful reporting in both General Ledger Accounting and Asset Accounting.

Using an example, you are guided through the entire process - from the definition of custom fields to reporting in Asset Accounting. This example additionally contains the substitution of a custom field with another field.

Step 1: Preparation - Define and Enable Custom Fields

In our example, we want to create two custom fields for the following purposes:

Custom Field,
Name

Custom Field,
Technical Name

Purpose

AUC Assignment

YY1_AUC_COBL

Have an input field for user input in the following apps:

  • Post General Journal Entries (F0718) and
  • Manage Supplier Invoice (F0859)

AUC User Plant

YY1_AUC_JE_ITEM

During the posting to the WBS element of an investment project, we want to transfer the content of the Plant field to this custom field. As a result, we have the information about the original plant available throughout the whole process and see its value in the settlement journal entry.

We define our custom fields in the Custom Fields app.

When defining the custom fields, we use the following business contexts:

  • Accounting: Coding Block (FINS_CODING_BLOCK) and
  • Accounting: Journal Entry Item (FINS_JOURNAL_ENTRY_ITEM)

After we have published the custom fields, the list of custom fields looks as shown in Figure 1.

Figure 1: List of custom fields with their business contexts (Custom Fields app)

We then need to enable the field for usage in our apps. We do this on the User Interfaces tab of the Custom Fields app.

  • To have the custom fields available in the posting apps and in the substitution app, the corresponding OData services need to be enabled.
  • To make the custom fields available in the reports of the Asset Transactions app, we at least need to enable the fields for the OData Service FAA_ASSET_TRANSACTIONS_SRV.

In our example, we enable our first custom field YY1_AUC_COBL for the input in both posting apps and the asset transactional reporting. This is shown in Figures 2a-d.

Figure 2a: Enabling custom field YY1_AUC_COBL for the Manage Journal Entries app

Figure 2b: Enabling custom field YY1_AUC_COBL for the apps Post General Journal Entries, Display Line Items in General Ledger, and the Journal CDS view

Figure 2c: Enabling custom field YY1_AUC_COBL for the app Manage Supplier Invoice

Figure 2d: Enabling custom field YY1_AUC_COBL for the Asset Transactions app

Furthermore, we enable our second custom field (YY1_AUC_JE_ITEM) for substitution and for reporting in the Asset Transactions app:

Figure 3a: Enabling custom field YY1_AUC_JE_ITEM for the app Manage Substitution and Validation Rules (F4406), Variant Journal Entries

Figure 3b: Same as figure 2d. - Enabling custom field YY1_AUC_JE_ITEM for the Asset Transactions app (F1614)

If we wanted to use the custom fields in further apps, we would have to enable the corresponding services, too.

Step 2: Preparation - Define Substitution Rule for Custom Field

We use the app Manage Substitution and Validation Rules (F4406), variant Journal Entries, to define the substitution for our second custom field YY1_AUC_JE_ITEM. During a cost posting to the WBS element, the system shall replace the custom field YY1_AUC_JE_ITEM with the Plant entry.

Figure 4a: Creating the substitution rule, entry screen

 

Figure 4b: Creating the substitution rule, detail screen

After we had created the substitution rule, we have activated it.

Step 3: Preparation - Create Investment Project and AUC for Event-Based Settlement

A final preparation step is still missing before we can use the custom fields in the settlement process: We create an investment project with a WBS element, assigning an event-based settlement profile.

We create the investment project IM_EB1 in the app Project Control - Enterprise Projects (F3215), assign the settlement profile EB0001, and then release the project.

Figure 5: Creating the investment project (in app Project Control - Enterprise Projects (F3215))

The system automatically creates a corresponding asset under construction (AuC).

We then create a settlement rule for final settlement from the AuC to the completed asset (1010-20000000-0).

Step 4: Posting - Make Postings with Custom Field Input

Substep 4-1: Post General Journal Entry

Since we have enabled the custom field YY1_AUC_COBL for the required OData services, we can enter values for this custom field in the posting apps when posting costs to the WBS element.

In our example, we make a posting in the app Post General Journal Entries (F0718) where we enter the following account assignments:

  • WBS element: IM_EB1
  • Custom field AUC Assignment (YY1_AUC_COBL): Input 1

Figure 6: Posting in app Post General Journal Entries (F0718)

Substep 4-2: Post Supplier Invoice

We make a second posting in the Manage Supplier Invoice app (F0859) where we enter the following account assignments:

  • WBS element: IM_EB1
  • Custom field AUC Assignment (YY1_AUC_COBL): Input 2

Figure 7: Posting in app Manage Supplier Invoice (F0859)

Substep 4-3: Post Purchase Order with 'Plant' Substitution and Subsequent Goods Receipt

We create a purchase order for our investment project (WBS element).

We do this in the app Manage Purchase Orders (F0842A), where we specify the following:

  • Account Assignment Category: Project (P)
  • Plant: New Plant FIO1 (FIO1)
  • WBS Element: IM_EB1

Figure 8: Creating a purchase order (in app Manage Purchase Orders (F0842A))

Later, we post the goods receipt that belongs to the purchase order. We do this in the app Post Goods Receipt for Purchasing Document (F0843); we specify the purchase order.

Figure 9: Posting the goods receipt (in app Post Goods Receipt for Purchasing Document (F0843))

The cost posting is then made to the WBS element IM_EB1. Since we have created the investment project (with WBS element IM_EB1) with an event-based settlement profile, the costs are settled directly to the AuC. The process flow is shown in figure 10.

Figure 10: Process flow from purchase order to AuC

As defined in the substitution rule, our second custom field YY1_AUC_JE_ITEM is replaced with the plant entry of the goods receipt journal entry (5000000001) and the custom field is also copied to the settlement journal entry (100000003).

Figure 11: Line items for the goods receipt journal entry (in app Post Goods Receipt for Purchasing Document (F0843))

Figure 12: Line items for the settlement journal entry (in app Manage Journal Entries (F0717A)

Step 5: Posting - Post Final Settlement to Completed Asset

During the settlement of the AuC to the final receiver asset (completed asset) 20000000-0, all custom field information is taken over to the line items for the AuC retirement posting on the one hand and to the line items for the asset acquisition (for the completed asset) on the other.

Figure 13: Posting of final settlement from AuC to completed asset

Step 6: Reporting

For reporting purposes, you can use the Asset Transaction app (F1614). In its view Journal Entry Tracing for Settlement, you can display the settlement values for both, for the AuC and for the completed asset including the custom field account assignments.

Figure 14: Reporting the settlement postings including the custom field account assignments (app Asset Transaction (F1614), view Journal Entry Tracing for Settlement)

Conclusion and More Information

Custom fields flow seamlessly through the entire settlement process, while data consistency is ensured. Whether you're tracking specific cost categories, plant assignments, or other custom-specific data, custom fields make it possible.

The setup of custom fields requires enabling OData services and may involve creating substitution rules, but once configured, the process runs smoothly and automatically.

Want to learn more about event-based settlement? - For more information, see the help portal documentation under Assets Under Construction: Management and Settlement, Event-Based Settlement | SAP Help Portal and Extensibility: Using Custom Fields with Event-Based Settlement

For general information about the enablement of custom fields in the accounting business contexts as well as for UI adaptions, see SAP Note 3448139.

entry.title: Issue with Bank Statement Processing Rules for Bank Fees
entry.content:
Dear SAP Support Team,

We are currently experiencing an issue with the processing rules configured for bank fees in bank statement uploads.

We have defined an automated rule for our client to enable automatic posting of bank fees during the bank statement import. The rule is set up with the following criteria:

  • Company Code: equal to 4400
  • Memo Line: contains (chinese characters)
  • House Bank: between BOC1 and BOC2
  • House Bank Account: between CNY1 and CNY2
  • House Bank Account: between EUR1 and EUR2
  • House Bank Account: between USD1 and USD2

The posting is configured to GL account 71000000 along with the relevant cost center.

However, we have observed that when the memo line contains the text "BANK CHARGES" (Latin characters), the system correctly posts the bank fees automatically. In contrast, when Chinese characters are used, the automatic posting is not triggered.

Could you please confirm whether processing rules for bank statements support the use of non-Latin (e.g., Chinese) characters in memo lines? Additionally, we would appreciate any guidance on how to enable correct processing in such cases.

Thank you in advance for your support.

Best regards,
Claudia

entry.title: Re: Create Maintenance Request App AdaptUI Notif Type Change
entry.content:
Dear @MuratTnrkt,

This is most likely not caused mainly by missing Y1 customizing. In standard Create Maintenance Request (F1511A), SAP states that the app supports selecting the notification type and that Y1 is only the default. SAP also documents a known symptom where the Notification Type field is not available at all and the app creates Y1 by default. Therefore this points more to a difference in the base app / runtime / adaptation-project state between the two projects rather than simply to missing notification type Y1 configuration. Missing Y1 can explain a later save error such as Notification type Y1 not defined but not necessarily why the editable field is absent in Adapt UI. SAP adaptation-project documentation also confirms that project behavior depends on the base app and whether the project is up to date with base app upgrades

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/2dfa044a255f49e89a3050daf3c61c11/5d2fbff31efc440b8200fbad95a68dfe.html

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003559950

Summary:

Missing Y1 may explain a save/defaulting problem, but the missing Notification Type field itself is more likely due to an app/runtime/adaptation-project difference

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Business Objects Enterprise 4.3 update from SP04 patc...
entry.content:
Hi Andrea,

You will need to make sure Windows Defender real-time protection truly disabled during install. Are exclusions set for:

    • D:\SAP BusinessObjects\
    • D:\SAP BusinessObjects\InstallData\ (InstallCache, tmp)
    • %ProgramData%\SAP BusinessObjects\ (if used)
    • %TEMP% and the installer’s extraction directory?

Make sure to stop all BO services (SIA, Tomcat) and verify no BO processes remain in Task Manager.

Disable Windows Search indexing for D:\SAP BusinessObjects\ and subfolders.

Confirm NTFS permissions and ownership:

  • Inherit permissions from D:\SAP BusinessObjects down to InstallData, InstallCache, tmp.
  • Ensure the installer account has Full Control.

Clear stale installer state:

  • Rename D:\SAP BusinessObjects\InstallData\InstallCache\ to InstallCache.bak and let the installer rebuild cache.
  • Ensure %TEMP% is accessible and not redirected to a restricted path.

Reboot the server before re-attempt to release file locks.

Disable Controlled Folder Access in Defender during the install .

Validate no other agents remain (backup, DLP, EDR remnants). If present, stop services during the upgrade window.

If it still fails:

Use Process Monitor (ProcMon) from Sysinternals during the upgrade, filtered to:

  • Path contains InstallData\tmp
  • Result is ACCESS DENIED

This will show you exactly which process is blocking the file operation.
